author: garyrong
content:
From my pov, both approaches are practical. 

In devp2p, the base protocol reserves baseProtocolLength (16) message IDs for the p2p transport layer, and the remaining IDs are available for upper-level protocols. Since message codes are represented as uint64, the ID space is theoretically more than sufficient.

With an increment-only approach, the implementation might be simpler (though I’m not entirely sure, since it's generally fine for two protocol versions to define messages with the same numeric ID). On the other hand, reusing IDs can look cleaner at the protocol specification level, since it avoids gaps in the message numbering.

I would prefer the reusing one.

author: nero_eth
content:

cc <@348512261955911691> <@429719902363058177>

author: mirgee
content:
> On the other hand, reusing IDs can look cleaner at the protocol specification level, since it avoids gaps in the message numbering.

Avoiding gaps makes a single version’s table look slightly tidier, but it makes the protocol harder to reason about across versions, because message IDs stop being stable identifiers and become version-relative.

If someone sees `0x08` in code, traces or metrics, increment-only numbering makes it easier to reason about what message type that refers to. With reuse, `0x08` is not a stable reference; you first need to know the negotiated protocol version.

Reusing freed IDs may remove gaps, but it can also make semantically related messages end up with arbitrary IDs, whereas increment-only approach preserves some historical or semantic structure.

I think that aliasing message IDs across versions is more error prone, whereas gaps are operationally harmless and ID space is abundant, so that seems like a bad trade.

author: matkt0
content:
A message code 0x08 always means the same thing, regardless of context. This makes debugging straightforward: logs, traces, and metrics become immediately readable without needing version context. It eliminates the risk of misinterpretation when reading code or analyzing network data.

And also removing gaps in numbering are purely cosmetic. they have no functional impact. But reusing IDs creates invisible technical debt: every retired message becomes a potential pitfall for future developers. Code is easier to read when the ID history is transparent and linear.

author: nero_eth
content: I agree with <@628018527470616606> that we don't need to rush things here. Clients can take their time for implementation and in a month or so, we can discuss that further, based on how far with the implementation clients are. If there are enough clients ready (or will be ready until the fork) we can still decide to ship it alongside glamsterdam - if not, nothing breaks.

author: garyrong
garyrong
content:
I think it would be preferable to roll out both the serving and syncing parts of snap/2 at the same time.

If snap/2 is deployed for serving only, it will be negotiated as the primary protocol version with all peers that support snap/2. In that case, it may become difficult to find snap/1 peers for syncing.

author: garyrong
content:
I also don't think it's necessary to roll out snap/2 across different client teams. 

For example, if Geth is the only client supporting snap/2, it can still use snap/2 as the primary syncing method by syncing from other Geth nodes that support it.

When connecting to peers from other clients, snap/1 will be negotiated instead, and Geth will still retain its serving capabilities.

author: nero_eth
content:
Awesome, thanks everyone for the feedback so far! This was helpful.
I'll then proceed with updating the EIP (assigning new Message IDs instead of reusing those of snap/1), and regarding the rollout we don't need to rush things: it be good if we have a simultanous rollout but not strictly necessary (esp. not for majority clients).
We can then revisite this in a month or so.
